retrieved_docs.title: Hepatitis B -- Evaluation -- Interpretation of Serologic Markers
retrieved_docs.content: Other markers are: Hepatitis B viral DNA is for detection of viral load. Hepatitis B genotype provides input about disease progression and response to interferons. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatitis B -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnosis of hepatitis B relies on the appropriate history/physical and evaluation of serum or viral biomarkers. Viral serology of hepatitis B is usually detectable 1-12 weeks after initial infection with the primary viral marker being hepatitis B surface antigen (HBsAg). The presence of HBsAg rarely persists beyond 6 months after infection and typically precedes detectable quantities of the corresponding antibody to surface antigen (Anti-HBsAg). The period of time between the disappearance of HBsAg and the appearance of Anti-HBsAg is termed “the window period” or “serological gap.” During the window period, other viral serology could also be undetectable. HBsAg is the first virological marker to be detected thanks to its exposure on the viral surface and is indicative of an acute infection. Immune-mediated destruction of the nucleocapsid allows exposure of core antigen (HBcAg) or e antigen (HBeAg) with subsequent antibody development. Liver enzymes are typically elevated within the latter part of the replicative phase on infection thanks to active inflammatory processes, otherwise, liver transaminases could also be within their reference ranges. Hence, liver transaminases should not be a sole guide to diagnosing suspected hepatitis B infection.

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatitis B -- Evaluation -- Interpretation of Serologic Markers
retrieved_docs.content: Following serologic markers are often tested: Hepatitis B surface antigen (HBsAg), antibody to Hepatitis B surface antigen (anti-HBs), Hepatitis B core Ab (Anti-HBc) IgM, Hepatitis B core Ab (Anti-HBc) IgG, Hepatitis B e antigen (HBeAg), and Hepatitis B e antibody (anti-HBe). [10] HBsAg: Acute infection (less than 6 months) or chronic infection (more than 6 months). Anti-HBs: Recovery from acute infection or immunity from vaccination. HBeAg: Mostly associated with high viral load. Anti-HBe: Low replicative phase. Anti-HBc IgM: Acute infection, an only marker present in the window period, can be present during exacerbation of chronic infection. Anti-HBc IgG: Exposure to infection, chronic infection (if present along with HBsAg), recovery from acute infection (if present with anti-HBs), if isolated presence, may represent occult infection.

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatitis B -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Persons with elevated ALT or AST of unknown origin.

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatitis B -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: All pregnant women and infants born to HBsAg-positive mothers.

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatitis B -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The presence of antibodies to HBsAg indicates immunized status while the presence of antibodies to HBeAg refers to a possible chronic infection state. Seroconversion refers to the transition between an acute, immune-active phase to an inactive carrier state and is marked by the spontaneous development of antibodies to HBeAg. Earlier seroconversion has been related to more favorable outcomes while later seroconversion, in conjunction with recurrent bouts of reactivation and remission, is more liable to complications like liver cirrhosis, thus resulting in poorer outcomes. [10] The persistence of serum HBsAg for a duration of 6 months or greater delineates acute hepatitis B infection from chronic hepatitis B infection. Following groups of people should be screened for hepatitis B: [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatitis C -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Further evaluation consists of checking the viral genotype, which is still important in choosing the most optimal regimen and predicting the response to therapy. Other baseline evaluations include testing for HIV, hepatitis B surface antigen, susceptibility to hepatitis A and hepatitis B virus infections, and screening for other underlying causes of liver disease such as autoimmune liver disease, hemochromatosis, and Wilson disease. Before determining the HCV treatment strategy, the next step is to stage the disease, utilizing liver biopsy (gold standard) or approved imaging modalities with or without noninvasive biomarkers. Lastly, these patients should also undergo variceal screening and screening for hepatocellular carcinoma. [9] [10] [11] Other studies include: INR, prothrombin time Complete blood count Liver function tests GFR Serology for other hepatic viruses Thyroid function Screening for drug abuse and alcohol Mental health assessment

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatitis B -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnosis of Hepatitis B is based on proper history taking, physical examination, laboratory works, and imaging.

retrieved_docs.title: Viral Hepatitis -- Evaluation -- Hepatitis D
retrieved_docs.content: HDV infection is diagnosed by checking for IgM and IgG antibodies to the HDV (anti-HDV). IgM antibody to HbcAg (anti-HBc) must be checked to differentiate a coinfection where the patients test positive for IgM anti-HBc and superinfection, where the patients test negative for IgM anti-HBc. (see Table. Hepatitis D Stages, Serological Markers, and the Three Infective Patterns). HDV RNA test can be done but is not routinely performed. [41]

retrieved_docs.title: Viral Hepatitis -- Evaluation -- Acute hepatitis B infection
retrieved_docs.content: The first serum marker to appear in a patient with an acute HBV infection is the HBsAg. This antigen indicates that the patient has hepatitis B viremia, but it does not differentiate between an acute versus chronic infection, especially in the absence of symptoms. When the patient exhibits signs of acute hepatitis, the presence of HBsAg strongly suggests acute HBV infection. However, it does not rule out chronic HBV infection with a flare or superinfection. HBsAg disappears approximately 6 months after the acute infection in those patients who clear the HBV. If HBsAg remains present for more than 6 months, it indicates a chronic infection. For those who clear the hepatitis B viremia, the antibody to HBsAg (anti-HBs) appears. This antibody may persist for a lifetime and offers immunity to the patients from subsequent exposure to HBV.

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatitis D -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: High-risk individuals (intravenous drug users, individuals from HDV-endemic areas) or those who present with severe course who have acute hepatitis B should be suspected of HDV coinfection. As mentioned, patients with coinfection have high titers of IgM anti-HBc. In these individuals, HDV markers can come before or after HBV markers. Occasionally, patients who present during the second phase of biphasic hepatitis seroconvert to anti-HBs, but they could still test positive for high titer IgM anti-HBc. When the etiology of hepatitis is undetermined in chronic HBV carriers, HDV testing should be performed to rule out an acute HDV superinfection. Distinguishing between HDV superinfection and HBV/HDV coinfection can be difficult when superinfection occurs in unidentified chronic HBV carriers.

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatitis B -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Blood, plasma, organ, tissues, or semen donors.

retrieved_docs.title: Viral Hepatitis -- Evaluation -- Chronic Hepatitis B infection
retrieved_docs.content: Similarly, HBV DNA may or may not be detectable, but high levels can indicate active chronic hepatitis. Patients with chronic HBV infection usually have an absence of anti-HBs. Still, the presence of anti-HBs with positive HBsAg in patients with chronic HBV infection indicates that the antibody was unsuccessful in inducing viral clearance.

retrieved_docs.title: Viral Hepatitis -- Evaluation -- Acute hepatitis B infection
retrieved_docs.content: Hepatitis B e antigen (HBeAg) is also present early in the infection and indicates that the virus is replicating. After the viral replication diminishes, HBeAg becomes undetectable, and antibodies to HBeAg (anti-HBe) appear and can persist in the blood indefinitely (see Table. Interpretation of Hepatitis B Screening Results).

retrieved_docs.title: Viral Hepatitis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: In addition to these initial routine laboratory tests, other specific tests are available to evaluate for the type of viral hepatitis as follows:

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatitis D -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: In response to the HDAg, antibodies of the immunoglobulin M (IgM) and immunoglobulin G (IgG class) (anti-HDV) are produced. The 3 infective patterns (acute HDV/HBV coinfection, acute HDV superinfection, and chronic HDV infection) vary concerning appearance and levels of HDV RNA, HDAg, and anti-HDV as well as HBV markers (see Image. Hepatitis D Stages and Serological Markers). As HDV depends on HBV, the presence of HBsAg is essential for diagnosis. Additionally, IgM antibody to hepatitis B core antigen (IgM anti-HBc) is necessary to diagnose acute HBV/HDV coinfection. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1856
retrieved_docs.content: الاعتماد على الكحول (alcohol dependence) is clinically associated with اِلْتِهَابُ الكَبِد (liver inflammation), indicating that chronic alcohol use can contribute to inflammatory damage of the liver. Hepatitis is classified as اِلْتِهَابُ الكَبِد, meaning that hepatitis represents a specific type of liver inflammation often caused by viral infection, autoimmune processes, or toxic insults. Together, these relationships highlight that liver inflammation can arise both from alcohol dependence and from hepatitis, underscoring the importance of evaluating alcohol use and identifying hepatitis when assessing patients with hepatic inflammatory conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2675
retrieved_docs.content: Hepatitis is the clinical term that classifies the condition known in Arabic as اِلْتِهَابُ الكَبِد, which refers to inflammation of the liver.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2620
retrieved_docs.content: Alcoholic hepatitis (تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ) is a liver disorder caused by excessive alcohol consumption that can progress to hepatic cirrhosis (تَشَمُّع كَبِدي). This relationship highlights the potential evolution from an inflammatory, potentially reversible condition to an irreversible stage of extensive liver scarring, emphasizing the importance of early detection and treatment of alcoholic hepatitis to prevent the development of cirrhosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2341
retrieved_docs.content: Failure (biologic function) is associated with الفَشَلٌ الكَبِدِيُّ الكُلْوِيّ, indicating that a general loss of biological function can be linked to hepatorenal failure—a severe condition in which both liver and kidney functions are compromised. Recognizing this association is important for clinicians, as it highlights the need to assess and manage systemic organ failure comprehensively in affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 483
retrieved_docs.content: Antivirals for treatment of HCV infections are direct acting antivirals, indicating that these medications specifically target viral proteins essential for hepatitis C virus replication.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2676
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of the liver and intrahepatic bile duct manifests as سرطان الخلايا الكبدية (liver cell carcinoma), which is broadly related to مَرَض كَبِدِيّ صَفْراوِيّ (jaundiced liver disease). This connection indicates that hepatocellular carcinoma often presents within the spectrum of liver diseases that cause jaundice, highlighting the importance of recognizing jaundice as a potential clinical clue to underlying malignant liver pathology. Understanding this relationship aids clinicians in early detection, accurate diagnosis, and appropriate management of patients with liver‑related malignancies and associated jaundice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 2 -- Community 1151
retrieved_docs.content: Hepatitis (اِلْتِهَابُ الكَبِد) is classified as liver inflammation. It is clinically associated with both alcohol‑abstinence syndrome (متلازمة الامتناع عن تعاطي الكحول) and hepatocellular carcinoma (سرطان الخلايا الكبدية). Hepatocellular carcinoma is a manifestation of cancer of the liver and intrahepatic bile duct and also has a broader relationship with cholestatic liver disease (مَرَض كَبِدِيّ صَفْراوِيّ). These interconnections highlight the role of hepatitis in the development of liver cancer and related hepatic disorders, emphasizing the importance of monitoring and managing hepatitis to prevent progression to malignancy and other liver complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 210
retrieved_docs.content: modalities, procedural interventions, and disease processes are anatomically and clinically related, providing a comprehensive framework for the evaluation and management of abdominal, hepatic, and biliary disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2074
retrieved_docs.content: Hepatitis, which is classified as اِلْتِهَابُ الكَبِد (liver inflammation), is clinically associated with متلازمة الامتناع عن تعاطي الكحول (alcohol withdrawal syndrome). This relationship indicates that patients experiencing alcohol abstinence may also develop or have concurrent liver inflammation, underscoring the importance of monitoring hepatic function and providing appropriate liver‑focused care during the management of alcohol withdrawal.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 369
retrieved_docs.content: Ribavirin is an antiviral medication that belongs to the class of antivirals used for the treatment of hepatitis C virus (HCV) infections, making it a member of the therapeutic group “Antivirals for treatment of HCV infections.”

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2274
retrieved_docs.content: Alcoholic cirrhosis (تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ) is a specific subtype of liver cirrhosis (تَشَمُّع كَبِدي). Liver cirrhosis, in turn, is clinically associated with esophageal variceal bleeding (نزيف دَوالِي المريء). Recognizing that patients with alcoholic cirrhosis develop the broader condition of liver cirrhosis helps clinicians anticipate the high risk of variceal hemorrhage, prompting appropriate screening, monitoring, and preventive interventions to improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 185
retrieved_docs.content: (نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ). Laboratory findings that fall below reference ranges, particularly low total hemoglobin concentration, are interpreted as indicative of hemolytic or perinatal jaundice, underscoring the diagnostic importance of quantitative testing in this age group. Collectively, these relationships illustrate that the neonatal period serves as a critical temporal nexus linking infectious, metabolic, hematologic, and hemorrhagic conditions—many organized under “other perinatal conditions” or “hemolytic‑jaundice” categories—and that recognizing these interconnections is essential for accurate assessment, diagnosis, and management of newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1206
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of liver and intrahepatic bile duct is a malignant condition that manifests as سرطان الخلايا الكبدية (liver cell cancer). Hepatitis (اِلْتِهَابُ الكَبِد), which is classified as liver inflammation, is clinically associated with سرطان الخلايا الكبدية, indicating that patients with hepatitis may have an increased risk of developing this type of liver cancer. Moreover, سرطان الخلايا الكبدية has a broader relationship with مَرَض كَبِدِيّ صَفْراوِيّ, a jaundiced liver disease, suggesting that liver cell cancer can be part of or contribute to the clinical spectrum of jaundice‑related hepatic disorders. Recognizing the interconnections among cancer of liver and intrahepatic bile duct, hepatitis, and مَرَض كَبِدِيّ صَفْراوِيّ is essential for comprehensive diagnosis and management of hepatic pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 184
retrieved_docs.content: liver, and biliary system; the utility of ultrasonographic techniques for biliary and hepatic pathology; the role of substances as causative agents for renal and hepatic toxicities; and the liver’s essential functions in detoxification, inflammation, malignancy, and both diagnostic and therapeutic clinical pathways.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1825
retrieved_docs.content: Immunity disorders are classified as a subset of نقص غلوبيولين الدم, which refers to a deficiency of blood globulins. This globulin deficiency results in reduced levels of immunoglobulins, compromising the body’s immune defenses and predisposing individuals to infections and other immune‑related complications. Recognizing immunity disorders within the broader context of نقص غلوبيولين الدم helps clinicians understand the underlying immunological deficit and guides appropriate diagnostic evaluation and therapeutic strategies aimed at restoring adequate immune function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1324
retrieved_docs.content: Clindamycin is contraindicated in patients with hepatic failure (الفَشَلٌ الكَبِدِيّ), meaning the drug should not be administered to individuals suffering from liver failure due to the risk of worsening hepatic function.
